an8.13:1.1: “Endowed with eight qualities, a king’s auspicious thoroughbred steed is worthy of a king, is a king’s asset, counts as a very limb of his king.
an8.13:1.3: [1] “There is the case where a king’s auspicious thoroughbred steed is well-born on both sides,
an8.13:1.5: he is born in the country where other auspicious thoroughbred steeds are born.
an8.13:1.8: he eats it carefully, without scattering it around.
an8.13:1.9: [3] “He feels disgust at sitting or lying down in urine or excrement.
an8.13:1.10: [4] “He is composed & easy to live with, and doesn’t harass the other horses.
an8.13:1.11: [5] “Whatever tricks or deceits or wiles or subterfuges he has, he shows them as they actually are to his trainer,
an8.13:1.12: so that his trainer can try to straighten them out.
an8.13:1.13: [6] “When in harness he gives rise to the thought,
an8.13:1.14: ‘Whether the other horses want to pull or not, I’ll pull here.’
an8.13:1.15: [7] “When going, he goes the straight path.
an8.13:1.16: [8] “He is steadfast and remains steadfast to the end of life & death.
an8.13:1.17: “Endowed with these eight qualities, a king’s auspicious thoroughbred steed is worthy of a king, is a king’s asset, counts as a very limb of his king.
an8.13:2.1: “In the same way, a monk endowed with eight qualities is deserving of gifts, deserving of hospitality, deserving of offerings, deserving of respect, an incomparable field of merit for the world.
an8.13:2.3: [1] “There is the case where a monk is virtuous. He dwells restrained in accordance with the Pāṭimokkha, consummate in his behavior & sphere of activity. He trains himself, having undertaken the training rules, seeing danger in the slightest faults.
an8.13:2.6: he eats it carefully, without complaining.
an8.13:2.7: [3] “He feels disgust at bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, mental misconduct,
an8.13:2.8: at the development of evil, unskillful (mental) qualities.
an8.13:2.9: [4] “He is composed & easy to live with, and doesn’t harass the other monks.
an8.13:2.10: [5] “Whatever tricks or deceits or wiles or subterfuges he has, he shows them as they actually are to the Teacher or to his observant companions in the holy life,
an8.13:2.11: so that the Teacher or his observant companions in the holy life can try to straighten them out.
an8.13:2.12: [6] “When in training he gives rise to the thought,
an8.13:2.13: ‘Whether the other monks want to train or not, I’ll train here.’
an8.13:2.14: [7] “When going, he goes the straight path;
an8.13:2.15: here the straight path is this:
an8.13:2.16: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
an8.13:2.18: ‘Gladly would I let the flesh & blood in my body dry up, leaving just the skin, tendons, & bones, but if I have not attained what can be reached through human steadfastness, human persistence, human striving, there will be no relaxing my persistence.’”
an8.13:2.19: “Endowed with these eight qualities, a monk is deserving of gifts, deserving of hospitality, deserving of offerings, deserving of respect, an incomparable field of merit for the world.”
